subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Logical progression from introduction through conclusion
Clear use of headings, tables, figures to structure the report
Relevant content focused on the research question
weaknesses: Abruptly cut aim and conclusion statements disrupt flow
Repetitive explanations and long computations reduce conciseness
Inconsistent figure captions and page numbering hamper readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is coherent and organized but includes redundancies, incomplete sentences and formatting inconsistencies that prevent it from reaching a higher level of conciseness and polish. Overall performance aligns with band-2 descriptors.

strengths: Correct typesetting of core equations with defined variables
Effective use of multiple representations (graphs, code, sketches)
Logical sequence of derivations supporting the argument
weaknesses: Notation inconsistencies (e.g. ÎÎ½ vs Îv, gâ value)
Figures lack detailed captions or scales
Some algebraic steps are compressed, requiring reader inference
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical language and representations are used appropriately, but inconsistent notation and occasional unexplained steps limit clarity, placing the work in the mid-band for communication.

strengths: Clear link to personal interests and career aspirations
Studentâs choice of hybrid propulsion model shows individual initiative
Integration of coding and cost estimation demonstrates creativity
weaknesses: Approach remains within conventional methods without novel optimisation
Brief treatment of spiral-trajectory modelling limits depth of personal contribution
Further extension or unique viewpoint could strengthen engagement
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration displays a solid level of independent thinking and a personal approach through modelling decisions and coding, consistent with band-2 performance in engagement.

strengths: Discussion of speed vs cost trade-offs and mission limitations
Identification of modelling assumptions and their implications
Suggestions for future improvements present in conclusion
weaknesses: Reflection remains qualitative, lacking quantitative sensitivity or error analysis
No deep critical appraisal of key approximations (e.g. variable acceleration)
Actionable next steps are suggested but not developed in current work
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student offers meaningful reflection on results and assumptions, acknowledging limitations and proposing general improvements, which aligns with band-2 descriptors for reflection.

strengths: Mathematics is directly relevant and applied purposefully to the research question
Use of Keplerian dynamics, Tsiolkovsky equation and numerical integration is commensurate with course level
Breadth of mathematical techniques (algebra, calculus, numerical methods) is handled effectively
weaknesses: Minor inaccuracies in constants (gâ value) and unexplained symbols (constant c)
Occasional unit-conversion steps are abbreviated and need clearer exposition
Sign conventions in integrals should be checked for consistency
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration employs relevant mathematics effectively and at the appropriate level, demonstrating sound understanding. Minor technical errors prevent it from reaching the highest band but justify a band-4 award.

strandSelections.reasoning: Derivations are set out in a logical sequence and most steps are explained. Occasional algebraic leaps (e.g. integration of sail equation) require the reader to fill gaps, but overall clarity is good, matching the third band descriptor.

Calculations are mostly correct and coherent, but several inaccuracies appear (incorrect gâ value, minor unit slips, unexplained constant c in sail velocity equation). These indicate good but not thorough understanding, matching the third band descriptor.

The student employs multiple forms: hand-drawn orbital sketches, Matplotlib graphs, a variable table and Python code. Each representation supports the mathematics yet some figures lack scales or detailed captions, limiting them to an âeffectiveâ rather than âhighly effectiveâ level.

The investigation is easy to follow from introduction to conclusion. Each section (aim, conventional transfer, solar-sail model, cost comparison, conclusion) builds on the previous one, showing a logical thread of inquiry. Although a few jumps occur when the student moves from analytical to numerical work, the reader can still retrace the argument without external sources. Minor lapses in linking ideas (e.g. cost equation derivation) prevent the work from reaching the âexcellentâ descriptor.

Developing Python code, estimating material costs and combining two propulsion techniques demonstrate creativity and initiative beyond a purely theoretical treatment. Still, the creativity is moderateâthe spiral-trajectory section is brief and not rigorously solvedâso the work fits the âgoodâ rather than âexceptionalâ descriptor.

Results are discussed (solar sail saves ~40 days, fuel cost scales linearly), and limitations are acknowledged. Analysis is thoughtful but not âdeep and insightfulâ; alternative parameter sets or error margins are absent, placing the work in the middle band.

The conclusion weighs speed versus cost and outlines future improvements, providing a clear evaluation with some implications. However, the evaluation is qualitative and lacks quantitative robustness, aligning with a level-2 descriptor.

The student reflects on modelling assumptions and mission trade-offs, showing meaningful engagement with the results. Reflection is present throughout, but depth is limited; for example, no quantitative sensitivity test accompanies the stated assumptions.

Equations are typeset correctly and variables are defined in a table. Standard notation (Îv, a, r) is mostly used consistently. Small slips such as mixing gâ = 9.18 m sâ»Â² and unconventional symbols (ÎÎ½) appear, so the usage is âmostly consistentâ rather than fully accurate.

The narrative references the studentâs career interests and justifies modelling decisions (e.g. why hybrid propulsion). This personalised direction is clear, yet the overall approach remains conventional, corresponding to the second band.

Most material is relevant to the research question, but the write-up includes repetitive explanations (e.g. duplicate inverse-square discussion) and long price calculations that add little mathematical insight. Wordiness and occasional digressions reduce overall conciseness, placing the strand in the middle band.

All mathematical content (Keplerâs law, vis-viva, Tsiolkovsky equation, inverse-square radiation, basic numerical integration) is directly relevant to estimating journey time and cost. The mathematics is applied purposefully rather than included for show, satisfying the âused effectivelyâ descriptor.

Choosing an original, topical question and building a hybrid transfer model shows significant independent thought. However, the modelling follows well-known textbook mechanics and does not extend into novel optimisation, so the evidence is solid but not outstanding.

Headings, tables, figures and an appendix give the report a recognisable introduction-body-conclusion framework. Code is separated into an appendix and diagrams are referenced in the text. Nevertheless, page numbering and figure captions are inconsistent and some code blocks break the narrative flow, so the organisation, while clear, is not exceptional.

Keplerian dynamics and logarithmic rocket equation are well within HL level and are handled with appropriate algebra/calculus. Numerical integration via SciPy is also commensurate with course level. The breadth and depth justify a band-4 selection.

annotations.comment: Assumptions are clearly listed, which aids the readerâs understanding. However, consider discussing the impact of each assumption numerically.
The sail assumptions omit modeling reorientation delays, which would reduce average thrust. Reflect on the potential time penalty.
The unit conversion to meters is rapid; show the arithmetic steps to reinforce the method and maintain clarity.
The introduction effectively establishes the context and personal interest in space exploration, engaging the reader and linking to the studentâs career aspirations.
The aim statement ends abruptly mid-sentence. Complete the thought on real-world incorporation and ensure each sentence is fully expressed.
Consider splitting the long introductory paragraph into two for conciseness and to improve readability by focusing separately on context and aim.
The variables table uses overarrow notation for velocities but does not define vector direction clearly. Add a note on vector orientation.
Research question is broad; tighten the scope by specifying which mission constraints or sail parameters will be varied.
The variable table spans a large area without row shading or alternating lines, making it harder to read. Use styling to improve clarity.
The Iââ and gâ variables table uses unconventional subscripts (e.g., g0). Use standard notation $$g_0$$ for consistency.
Figure 1 is referenced but the caption is too brief; include scale and label axes to make it self-contained.
The semi-major axis equation $$a=\frac{r_E+r_M}{2}$$ is correct but lacks reminder of variable definitions; briefly restate $$r_E,r_M$$.
Keplerâs Third Law is well applied, but the gravitational parameter $$GM$$ should be defined with units in the same context.
The calculated transfer time is correct but spell consistency for âHohmannâ must be maintained throughout (misspelled once as âHoffmannâ).
In the velocity equation $$v=\sqrt{GM/r}$$, ensure you consistently use the same symbol (you switch between $$v_h$$ and $$v_b$$).
The Mars velocity calculation reuses Earth's radius in the formula header; confirm the correct $$r$$ value is applied to avoid confusion.
Break the derivation of periapsis velocity into smaller algebraic steps rather than combining multiple operations in one expression.
Express the symbolic form $$91v_{burn1}=v_p-v_E$$ before numeric substitution to reinforce understanding of the algebraic procedure.
The cost assumption list uses â\u0394\u03BDâ instead of â\u0394vâ. Use consistent notation to avoid reader confusion.
The Tsiolkovsky equation uses $$g_o=9.18\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$$; correct this to the standard $$9.81\,\mathrm{m/s^2}$$.
When rearranging the rocket equation to solve for $$m_{initial}$$, clearly define the variable â¢x (payload mass) before use.
The cost function $$C(x)=(1.4377x\times\$2.50)+(2.8755x\times\$0.27)$$ mixes math and currency symbols; separate units from the formula for clarity.
The solar sail acceleration model $$a(r)=\frac{2\eta P_0A}{mr^2}$$ is well presented, but define each symbol immediately after the equation.
Two identical integral expressions appear back-to-back; remove redundancy to maintain conciseness and logical flow.
The constant \u201Cc\u201D appears in $$v=\sqrt{2c-\frac{4\eta P_0A}{mr}}$$ without explanation. Define \u201Cc\u201D or derive it earlier.
Figure illustrating spiral trajectory lacks a caption; add a descriptive title and axis labels to support understanding.
In the integration of gravitational and sail forces, check sign conventions; ensure the resultant expression correctly reflects opposing directions.
The kinematic equation $$v^2=v_0^2+2a\Delta x$$ assumes constant acceleration but here \u201Ca\u201D depends on r; discuss this approximation.
The conclusion section begins but is abruptly cut off mid-sentence; ensure a clear, complete summary that reflects on results and implications.
The personal reflection section lists limitations but does not propose how they could be addressed in the current work. Add actionable next steps.
The works cited page has inconsistent formatting (varying use of italics and quotation marks). Standardize citations according to a style guide.
